statement.markdown: You are given a 2D grid of size R x C containing chocolates. Player 1 starts at the top-left cell (0, 0) and Player 2 starts at the top-right cell (0, C-1). From any cell (r, c), a player can move to (r+1, c-1), (r+1, c), or (r+1, c+1). If both players land on the same cell, only one of them collects the chocolates in that cell. Find the maximum chocolates they can collect together when they reach the bottom row (R-1).
statement.inputFormat:
The first line contains two integers R and C, representing the number of rows and columns respectively.
Each of the next R lines contains C space-separated integers representing the chocolates in each cell.
statement.outputFormat: A single integer representing the maximum chocolates collected.

constraints.description: Number of chocolates in each cell

examples.explanation:
Player 1 path: (0,0) [2] -> (1,1) [4] -> (2,1) [6]. Total = 12.
Player 2 path: (0,3) [2] -> (1,2) [2] -> (2,3) [5]. Total = 9.
Total chocolates collected = 12 + 9 = 21.
Both players collect all chocolates in both rows. Total = 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 = 4.

hints.text: The problem involves finding the number of unique paths in a grid with restrictions on movement and obstacles.
The movement is restricted to only down or right, which suggests a dynamic programming approach might be suitable.
The presence of obstacles (represented by 1) means that some cells cannot be part of a valid path, and this needs to be accounted for when calculating the number of paths.

editorial.title: Ninja and His Friends: A Dynamic Programming Approach
editorial.intuition: The problem requires finding the maximum number of chocolates that can be collected by two ninjas moving through a grid. The key observation is that the optimal solution can be built up by considering the maximum number of chocolates that can be collected up to each cell by the two ninjas.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force approach would involve trying all possible paths for the two ninjas, which would result in an exponential time complexity. This approach is correct but slow, with a time complexity of O(3^R * C^2), making it impractical for large inputs.
editorial.betterSolution: The optimal approach uses dynamic programming to build up a table of maximum chocolates that can be collected up to each cell. This approach takes advantage of the fact that the maximum number of chocolates that can be collected up to a cell only depends on the maximum number of chocolates that can be collected up to the previous cells.
editorial.optimalSolution: The dynamic programming approach has a time complexity of O(R * C^2) and a space complexity of O(C^2), making it much more efficient than the brute force approach.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the dynamic programming approach can be shown by induction. The base case is when R = 1, in which case the maximum number of chocolates that can be collected is simply the sum of the chocolates in the first row. For the inductive step, we assume that the maximum number of chocolates that can be collected up to each cell in the (R-1)th row is correct, and show that the maximum number of chocolates that can be collected up to each cell in the Rth row is also correct.
editorial.dryRun: A dry run of the dynamic programming approach with a sample test case can help verify its correctness. For example, with a 2x2 grid with chocolates [[1, 2], [3, 4]], the approach correctly returns 10 as the maximum number of chocolates that can be collected.
commonMistakes: Not considering all possible movements of the two ninjas
Not using dynamic programming to build up the table of maximum chocolates
Not handling the base case correctly
Not handling the inductive step correctly
Not considering the space complexity of the approach
Not verifying the correctness of the approach with test cases
